turns.text: Hello Allegra, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s… strange, really. Sitting here, staring at the test result, I feel like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff I already fell off once. Most people look at a positive pregnancy test and see a beginning. I look at mine and I see a countdown.

I remember everything so clearly from last time—the bright, sterile white of the hospital ceiling and the way my own body turned into a stranger. They told me I had eclampsia. It sounds like such a clean, academic word, doesn't it? But there was nothing clean about it. It was violent, and it was terrifying. Now that I’m here again, I feel like I’m wearing a target. My chart says "medium risk," but to the doctors, I know I’m just a ticking clock. I’ve read so much research—maybe too much—about how to have a natural, peaceful birth, but the shadow of that first experience makes it feel like I’m just waiting for my blood pressure to spike again.

Then there’s the day-to-day. Getting to the clinic in West Boylston without a car feels like a mountain I have to climb every few weeks. I’m exhausted, I’m scared, and frankly, I feel very much alone. I just want someone to look at me and see a mother, not a complication. Is that too much to ask?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly? I think I was naive. I spent so much time reading about midwives and holistic care—I really believed that if I educated myself enough, I could "outsmart" my medical history. I imagined a partnership, you know? A provider who would sit with me, acknowledge the trauma of the seizures, and help me build a plan that didn't just revolve around monitoring me like I’m a piece of malfunctioning equipment. I wanted a guide who respected my body's potential, not just its capacity to fail.

The reality… it’s been a cold bucket of water to the face. The care is so segmented. I show up to the clinic, usually after an hour of navigating buses and walking, and the provider barely has time to look up from their screen. They see "Eclampsia, G1" on the intake form, and their whole demeanor shifts. The warmth goes out of the room. It becomes this clinical, defensive sort of interaction. Everything is framed in terms of prevention and protocol, which I get—I know the risks better than anyone—but it feels like they’re managing a condition rather than supporting a woman. I’m just a box they need to check to make sure they aren’t liable. There’s no space for the fear I feel, or the fact that I’m doing this entirely on my own.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise, and honestly the most crushing one, was the immediate dismissal of my desire for a more natural approach. I had come in with a notebook full of research, hoping to have a real conversation about how we could manage my blood pressure without defaulting to the most aggressive interventions the moment I hit the hospital doors. I wanted to talk about autonomy, about the things I’d learned that might make this time different.

Instead, the provider looked at my chart, barely glanced at me, and essentially told me that my history "dictated the path." It wasn't a conversation; it was a lecture. They didn't even acknowledge the trauma of what happened before—they just saw a liability. I was shocked by how quickly my voice was erased. I felt like I was being told that because my body failed me once, I had lost the right to have a say in how it’s treated this time. 

I’d spent years dreaming of birth as this empowering, transformative space, and they made it sound like I was just a vessel for a high-risk diagnosis. That disconnect between my internal hope and their cold, robotic assessment was just… it was a gut punch. It’s hard to feel like a mother when the people who are supposed to help you don't see you as a person at all.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I thought I’d feel strong. That’s the irony, I suppose. I spent so many years reading journals, learning about the physiology of labor, and telling myself that knowledge was power. I fully expected that if I just did everything "right"—if I was prepared and informed—that I’d feel this sense of calm confidence. I thought I’d be able to walk into this pregnancy with my head held high, ready to prove that my body was capable of doing what it was designed to do.

But the reality? It’s not calm. It’s a low-grade, constant hum of terror. 

When I look at my reflection, I don’t see a woman embarking on a beautiful journey; I see a woman bracing for impact. It’s exhausting. Every time I get a headache or feel a little dizzy from the stress of it all, my heart starts racing because my brain immediately jumps to, *“Is this it? Is the seizure starting?”* I expected to feel like a participant in my own pregnancy, but instead, I feel like I’m a passenger in a car with no brakes. I’m mourning the pregnancy I thought I’d have—the one where you’re excited and glowing—and instead, I’m just trying to keep my head above water in this ocean of anxiety. It’s a very lonely, heavy way to live.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

It’s the simple absence of a witness. That’s what’s missing. 

I don't have a partner to hold my hand or to be the one to ask the hard questions when I’m too overwhelmed to speak. And when I sit in that exam room, the doctors are so focused on the *clinical* that they seem to forget I’m a human being carrying a very heavy psychological weight. I need someone to say, "I know you're terrified, and that makes sense." I need someone to acknowledge that the trauma of almost dying is still living in my cells. Instead, they just adjust the dosage of my aspirin and move on.

Beyond the medical side, I’m just... invisible. I don't have a support network to call when the fatigue sets in or when the reality of being a single mother starts to feel like a suffocating blanket. It’s not just about needing a ride to the clinic—though, God, that would be a start—it’s about needing someone to remind me that I’m more than just a pregnancy that might go wrong. There’s no one to tell me I’m brave, or to help me celebrate the tiny wins. I’m carrying all of this weight—the history, the bills, the appointments, the fear—entirely in my own head. It’s a very quiet, hollow kind of struggle.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I think I expected to find community—a place where other women who had been through "scary" pregnancies talked about the aftermath. I wanted stories about finding strength after trauma, or maybe even just practical tips on how to balance medical needs with that desire for a more autonomous, natural experience. I was looking for a bridge between the clinical, cold reality I knew and the peaceful, empowering experience I wanted.

But the internet is a polarized wasteland. On one side, you have the "perfect" pregnancy apps that are all neon colors, cute illustrations of fruit sizes, and toxic positivity. They tell you to "glow" and "enjoy every moment," which just makes me feel like a freak for being so scared. They completely ignore anyone with a medical history more complex than morning sickness. 

On the other side, you have the forums for high-risk pregnancies, which are just endless, terrifying threads of worst-case scenarios. It’s all fear-mongering and horror stories. None of it felt like *me*. None of it addressed how you advocate for yourself when you’re already exhausted from taking three buses just to get to a doctor who won't listen. It felt like I had to choose between being a "blissful" mom who ignores the facts or a "high-risk" patient who is treated like a disaster waiting to happen. There’s no middle ground for a woman like me who just wants to be human.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The gap is basically an ocean, and I’m the one drowning in the middle of it. 

The digital sources, the "community" forums, and the wellness blogs are all about reclaiming your birth experience—they emphasize informed consent, doulas, and how you have the right to push back against protocols. They make you feel like you are the captain of your own ship. They build up this idea that if you’re educated enough, you can advocate your way into a “gentle” birth, even if you’ve had a complicated history.

Then, I walk into the clinic, and the provider hands me a script that is the exact opposite. To them, my agency is a nuisance. They act like every question I ask—every request to avoid a certain intervention or to understand *why* a test is necessary—is a sign that I’m being "difficult" or "non-compliant." 

The apps tell me I’m a hero for wanting to advocate for my own body, but my doctor treats me like a threat to their standard of care. There’s no space to bridge that. The provider doesn’t see my research as valid preparation; they see it as me trying to interfere with their "proven" method of keeping me alive. It makes me feel like I’m constantly trapped between two worlds, and in both of them, I lose.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I suppose I expected a "bridge." Since I knew my history—the eclampsia, the seizures—I naively thought the healthcare system would have a protocol for pre-conception counseling that was actually... welcoming. I expected a provider to sit down with me before I even started trying, to talk about my blood pressure, my lifestyle, and how we could mitigate the risks together. I wanted someone to say, "Allegra, we know you’ve been through a nightmare, so here is a plan to keep you safe *and* supported." I wanted a team, a strategy, a partnership.

But when I actually tried to seek that out, it felt like I was being processed by a machine. There was no "preparing" me; there was just the documentation of my previous trauma. They didn't want to talk about how to keep me healthy; they wanted to list all the reasons why this pregnancy was inherently dangerous. It was never a discussion about wellness; it was an assessment of my flaws.

I walked in hoping for a strategist, and I walked out feeling like a liability. It felt as if my medical history was the only thing in the room, and my actual life, my exhaustion, and my desire for a different experience were just background noise they didn't have the time to process. They didn't prepare me—they just warned me.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

The idea that the system would "proactively reach out" is honestly laughable to me at this point. I have to fight for everything. Every appointment is a battle of logistics just to get there, and then once I'm in the room, I’m fighting to be heard over the sound of their pens scratching on my chart. 

Nothing is proactive. When I have questions about my medication or when I ask why we’re rushing toward certain interventions, I see the provider’s eyes glaze over. They don't want a partner; they want a compliant patient. I feel like I have to walk in there with my armor on, prepared to defend my choices, but I’m so tired. I’m exhausted from the physical side of being pregnant and the soul-crushing weight of being alone, and then I’m forced to play the role of the "difficult" patient just so I don't get lost in the shuffle.

Advocating for myself is a full-time job I didn't apply for, and the worst part is that doing it leaves me with even less energy to actually nurture myself or the baby. If I don't speak up, I’m just a risk factor. If I do speak up, I’m a problem. There is no version of this where I feel supported or safe. I’m just constantly on the defensive.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

It’s hard to talk about this because the reality is so different from the dream I held onto for years. Before I conceived, I imagined a partner as a stabilizer. I pictured someone who would sit at the kitchen table with me to look over my research, someone who would drive me to those appointments so I didn't have to navigate the bus while feeling like my head was spinning. I imagined a partner who would be the one to ask the doctors, "How is she feeling? What can we do to make this easier on her?" I really thought I’d have a teammate, someone to hold the emotional weight when I felt like I was sinking.

But that person isn't here. My reality is that I’m doing this completely on my own. There’s no one to lean on, no one to share the panic with at 3:00 a.m. when I start worrying about my blood pressure, and certainly no one to share the physical burden of getting to the clinic. 

The gap between that expectation—of being cared for—and the reality of being entirely solitary is profound. It’s like being a single parent from the very first day. It adds a layer of vulnerability that makes everything else—the medical risks, the transportation, the exhaustion—feel ten times heavier. I am both the patient and the primary advocate, with no one to hold the space for me.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

The burden has fallen entirely on me from the very first second. It’s not even a "disproportionate" split; it’s a total vacuum where someone else should be. 

Everything drives it—my poverty, my lack of a partner, the way the medical system is set up for people who have cars and husbands. Because I don't have those things, every single task is a monumental hurdle. I don't just have to manage my health; I have to manage the logistics of survival. I spend hours researching the bus schedules, then hours researching medical journals, then more time trying to figure out how to pay for the copays. 

It feels like the system is designed for a "perfect" patient—someone who is stable, connected, and supported. When you don't fit that profile, the burden of fixing the gaps falls 100% on your shoulders. If I don't advocate for myself, if I don't research the alternatives, if I don't map out the routes, *nothing* happens. I am the architect, the labor, and the quality control, and I am already so tired. It’s incredibly isolating to know that if I collapse—literally or figuratively—there is no one standing there to catch the pieces. I’m just trying to keep us both alive, and it feels like I’m doing it while running a marathon in the dark.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

When I chose to try again, I expected some form of "trauma-informed" care—a phrase I know sounds trendy, but to me, it meant someone sitting down and acknowledging that my body had been through a catastrophe. I expected them to talk to me about the *why* of the previous emergency, to help me heal the mental scars, and to offer a path forward that didn't just feel like a repeat of the same nightmare. I wanted them to validate that I was terrified, and to show me that they had a plan to prevent the same ending.

Instead, what I got was clinical indifference. There was no "care"—only monitoring. It was as if my previous trauma was just a footnote in a chart, something to check off so they could minimize their own liability. They didn't offer counseling, they didn't offer a mentor, and they certainly didn't offer any extra grace for the fact that I’m doing this while juggling life alone. 

It feels like they view me as a car that’s been in a wreck, and they’re just waiting for the next mechanical failure instead of trying to repair the frame so it can actually hold the weight of a pregnancy. They’re managing a statistic, not supporting a mother who survived a war.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

Every single time I walk into that clinic, I feel it. It’s in the way they flip past my history, skimming over the words "eclampsia" and "seizure" like they’re just typos on a grocery list. 

The most painful moment happened during my last visit. I tried to explain that I was struggling with the fear of it happening again—that the anxiety alone was spiking my blood pressure. I wanted them to hear me, to see the connection between my mental state and the physical risk. The provider didn't even pause. She just looked at my chart, adjusted my medication dosage, and said, "We’ll just keep a close eye on you." 

That’s all. *We’ll keep a close eye.* 

It felt like a slap in the face. My previous experience wasn't just a clinical complication; it was a near-death event that changed who I am. To them, it’s just a data point to monitor for the next breakdown. They aren't taking the *trauma* of the loss seriously; they’re only taking the *liability* of the risk seriously. They don't see me as a woman who survived a fire; they just see me as a building that’s prone to catching, and they’re waiting with their fire extinguishers, never once asking if I’m still afraid of the heat.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I expected a sanctuary. In my head, I kept picturing a quiet, warm room where I could sit down and really lay out everything—my fears, my specific history, the logistics of my daily struggle. I imagined a provider who would listen, maybe even put a hand on my shoulder and say, "We’ve got a plan for this. We aren't going to let what happened before happen again." I expected them to be as invested in my peace of mind as they were in my blood pressure.

The reality? It was anything but a sanctuary. It was cold, rushed, and profoundly mechanical. 

The environment was sterile fluorescent lights and thin paper gowns that felt like they were mocking my vulnerability. I was just another number in a queue. The person I saw wasn't a partner; they were a technician of risk. They didn't ask how I was *doing*; they asked what I had been eating and then immediately pivoted to the potential for pre-eclampsia. It was all "if this happens, do that" and "if you experience this, come straight to the ER." 

There was no space for the human element—not for the trauma, not for the loneliness, not for the fact that I’d had to take two transfers on the bus to get there. I felt like a broken piece of machinery being inspected by someone who just wanted to make sure I wasn't going to break down on their watch. It wasn't a conversation about a baby; it was an interrogation about a high-risk pregnancy. I left feeling lonelier than when I walked in.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—the one that left me reeling—was how completely and totally I was erased from the room. 

I’d spent so long preparing myself for the physical exam, rehearsing how I’d describe my history and what I wanted to talk about. I expected that even if we had different opinions on how to handle things, we would at least be *in the room together*. Instead, the doctor didn't look at me once for the first ten minutes. She looked at the screen, at the files, at the monitor, and then finally at my blood pressure cuff. 

It was like I had become a transparent vessel. 

I was surprised by how much power they had to just—*poof*—make me disappear. I had a question about how we could handle the stress of the clinic visits because I’m doing this alone and the travel is physically wrecking me. I wanted to see if we could consolidate appointments or find a way to minimize the stress on my heart. She cut me off mid-sentence to lecture me on "compliance" and "strict monitoring schedules."

I felt like an object on an assembly line. I went in hoping for a healthcare provider, but I realized I was just a case file. I was shocked, truly, at how little they cared about the person holding the baby. It wasn't just cold; it was alienating. I left the clinic and had to sit on a bench at the bus stop for twenty minutes just to stop my hands from shaking. I realized then that I wasn't just fighting the risks of eclampsia; I was fighting to be acknowledged as a human being.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected that the results would be the start of a conversation. I imagined sitting down, maybe at a desk, where the provider would walk me through the data. I thought they would explain what the numbers meant for me specifically—not just the averages, but what they meant for my health, my baby’s growth, and my risk profile. I thought we would weigh the information together, like partners, and that they would answer my questions with patience, even the ones that might seem a bit too technical.

The reality was a frantic, disconnected blur. It felt like I was being handed a set of puzzle pieces in the dark. 

Usually, I get a notification on a patient portal app, or a rushed, five-second phone call from a nurse who sounds like they’re reading from a script and just want to get to the next line. They never say, "How does this make you feel?" or "Do you have any concerns?" It’s all, "Your results are within normal limits, see you at your next appointment," or "Your blood pressure is slightly elevated; we need you to come in again." 

There’s no nuance. No context. I’m left to stare at these raw, clinical numbers on a screen, alone in my apartment, trying to reconcile them with the fear that has lived in my gut since the last time I was pregnant. It’s isolating. It’s like being given a roadmap for a journey, but the map is in a language I don’t fully understand, and nobody is willing to translate.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

The gap isn't just a small distance—it’s a canyon. The amount of information they give me is the bare minimum, just enough to satisfy a liability checklist. They’ll tell me, “Your protein levels are fine” or “Your blood pressure is X over Y,” but they never tell me *why* those numbers matter in the context of my specific history. I’m left to piece together the meaning myself, and since I don’t have a partner to talk through it with, I’m left staring at a screen until I’m shaking.

I need the *context*. I need them to explain the trend lines, to explain what they’re seeing that worries them, and to tell me what I can actually do to help stabilize things. But every time I try to bridge that gap, they shut it down, like they’re afraid I’m going to use the information to start "interfering" with their protocols. 

They provide the data, but they withhold the meaning. Without the meaning, the data is just a weapon. It’s like being told you’re in danger but being refused the tools to fix it. I’m an educated woman, I read the journals, I want to be a partner in this—but they treat me like a child who wouldn't understand, or worse, a burden who asks too many questions. It’s deeply insulting, and it keeps me up all night.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I expected a relationship. I really thought that if you were considered "medium risk," you would be assigned to a team—or better yet, a single midwife—who would actually *know* my chart, recognize my face when I walked in, and understand the weight of the anxiety I’m carrying. I wanted someone who, when I mentioned the stress of the bus ride from West Boylston, wouldn't just look at their watch, but would actually grasp that I’m struggling to keep my blood pressure steady while navigating these logistics alone.

The reality? It’s a revolving door. 

I almost never see the same person twice. Every appointment is like starting from square one. I have to re-explain my history, re-explain the eclampsia, and re-explain why I’m so nervous every time they pick up that cuff. I’m just a face in a waiting room full of other tired women. They look at the chart, they look at me, they see the "risk" label, and then they perform the standard procedure as if I’ve never been there before. 

There is no continuity. There is no one who is *my* person. I’m being passed around like a hot potato, and because there’s no consistent face, there’s no room for trust. How can I feel safe when the person responsible for my life doesn't even know my name without checking the screen?
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

The impact has been profound, and honestly, it’s corrosive. Practically, every appointment is a battle of repeating myself. I’m exhausted, I’m nauseous, and I’ve just spent over an hour on the bus—the last thing I have the energy for is to perform a retelling of my own trauma to a stranger who clearly hasn't bothered to read my chart before walking in the door. I’m not just the patient; I’m the record-keeper. I feel like I’m constantly auditing my own medical history just to make sure they don’t miss something critical.

Emotionally? It’s devastating. Trust requires a witness, and when I have a different provider every time, I don’t have a witness. I have a revolving door of people who see me as a set of symptoms, not a woman. Every time I walk in and realize it’s someone new, I feel a piece of my hope shrink. I have to guard myself, hold back my questions, and perform the role of the “compliant patient” again because I don’t have the energy to build rapport from scratch every three weeks. I feel so profoundly alone in this. It’s like I’m standing in a crowded room, but there isn't a single person there who actually knows what’s at stake for me. It’s not just fragmented care—it’s dehumanizing.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected those examinations to be a form of support. I thought, *Okay, my body is doing this heavy, terrifying work,* and I imagined the providers would approach those physical check-ups as a way of affirming that we were okay. I expected them to touch me with care, to talk to me about what was happening, and to help me feel connected to the baby growing inside me.

The reality? Those physical checks are essentially a hostile assessment of a broken object. 

It’s just... measure the bump, listen to the heart, wrap the cuff around my arm, and silence. It’s clinical to the point of being violent. When they do these things, they’re looking for evidence of failure. They aren't checking on *me*; they’re checking to see if the "risk" has manifested into a problem. It feels so intrusive. 

I’m sitting there, exposed, with my heart beating out of my chest, and they’re so focused on the blood pressure reading that they don’t notice my trembling hands or the way I hold my breath. They treat my body like it’s a site of impending disaster, not a home for my child. It makes me feel ashamed of my own skin, like my body is something I have to apologize for rather than something I’m carefully trying to protect. It’s lonely, and it’s deeply dehumanizing.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

It was during a routine check-up, around twelve weeks. The provider—someone I hadn't seen before—went to take my blood pressure. I was already shaky from the long trip on the bus, and I told her, "I'm a little anxious today, can we just take a breath for a second before we do this?" 

She didn't even look up from her screen. She just kept wrapping that cuff around my arm, tighter than it needed to be, and said, "It’s standard procedure. We need a baseline."

When the machine started whirring and tightening, I felt that familiar, cold grip of panic. My heart started thudding against my ribs, and she just watched the monitor, waiting for the numbers. When it spiked—obviously, because I was terrified—she sighed. It wasn't a sigh of empathy; it was the sigh of someone who had just found exactly what they expected to find: a problematic patient. 

She started lecturing me about "stress management" and "compliance," like I wasn't just a person doing their best to survive. She talked *about* my body as if I wasn't sitting right there, explaining how my blood pressure was "concerning" and "unstable." It was a complete violation. I felt small, exposed, and entirely dismissed. They never once asked what made me feel safe, or how they could help me lower my guard. They just confirmed their own bias that I was a liability. It made me feel like my own skin was an enemy I couldn't escape.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I expected a plan that acknowledged the complexity of my situation. Since I’ve survived eclampsia, I thought the medical system would treat my body with a kind of cautious reverence—that they would offer me resources for stress management, nutritional guidance, or at least someone to talk to about the *fear* of recurrence, because I know that anxiety directly impacts my blood pressure. I imagined a team that would help me navigate the "medium risk" label with a sense of partnership.

The reality is that they don't *manage* the condition; they just *police* it. 

It feels like every interaction is a threat. If my numbers aren't perfect, they don't offer support or solutions—they offer lectures on my failures. They treat my body’s struggle as a sign of my lack of self-discipline. It’s like they assume I’m not trying hard enough, as if I’m not spending every waking hour trying to keep my stress down and my body safe. 

There is no room for the reality of my life—the fact that I’m alone, that I’m exhausted, that I’m navigating public transit just to get to their office. They demand perfection from a woman who has no support, and when I can’t deliver that perfection, they act as if I’m doing something wrong on purpose. It’s not "managing" a health condition; it’s a constant, demoralizing test I feel like I’m failing every day.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

It’s the fundamental, crushing lack of humanity in the support. 

Everything is missing. 

Information? I get cold, detached data without context, leaving me to spiral in the dark. 

Emotional acknowledgement? That’s entirely absent. No one asks how I’m *carrying* this trauma, let alone how I’m carrying the pregnancy. They see a "medium risk" label and move immediately to policing my metrics, as if I’m not a person who has already walked through the fire of eclampsia. 

Practical help? That’s where the systemic failure is most obvious. I am a single woman in West Boylston without a car, and they act like my logistics are an annoyance I’ve brought upon myself. I have to fight for my health while fighting for my basic mobility, and not one single provider has ever asked, “How do you get here? Do you need a social worker to help with transportation or resources?” 

They don’t see the barriers; they only see the patient’s refusal to hurdle them. I am expected to perform like a woman with a stable partner, a support network, and a reliable car, and when I show up exhausted and alone, they treat me like I’m failing them. I need a hand to hold, a ride that doesn't cost me four hours of my day, and a provider who acknowledges that I am a human being trying to do the impossible alone.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that, given my history of eclampsia—a life-threatening condition—they would have a clear, direct line for me to use if I felt like something was "off." I thought I’d be able to talk to a person who understood the urgency of my anxiety, someone who could tell me whether to breathe through it or head straight to the hospital. I expected them to be as vigilant about my safety as I am.

The reality is like trying to yell across a canyon. 

When I call, I don't get a provider; I get a phone tree, then a receptionist who acts like I’m a nuisance for asking a question, and finally a nurse who reads from a standard protocol. If I’m lucky, I get a call back hours later, usually after I’ve already spent the time oscillating between panic and trying to talk myself down. They treat every concern like a logistical scheduling conflict rather than a potential medical emergency. 

When I actually *did* have a scare last week—my vision got a little blurry and my headache wouldn't quit—I was told to "monitor it" and call back if it got worse. There was no proactive care, no "come in right now just to be safe." It’s entirely on me to decide what’s an emergency. If I wait too long, I’m "non-compliant." If I go to the ER unnecessarily, I’m "wasting resources." I’m left to navigate this alone, and the system is not built to support me—it’s built to avoid me until I’m a full-blown crisis.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Yes. It happens more than I want to admit, and every time I delay, I feel a cold knot of dread in my stomach because I *know* better. I know the signs of eclampsia. I know the stakes. But the effort to actually get help is so agonizing that my brain starts playing these dangerous games: *"Maybe it's just stress. Maybe you're just exhausted from the bus. Don't be that 'difficult' patient again."*

Last week, I had that persistent, dull throb behind my eyes—the kind I remember right before the world went black the last time. My first instinct was to call the clinic, but then I thought about the hour-long trip. I thought about the nurse who would likely sigh, tell me to drink more water, and make me feel like I was wasting her time for being "anxious." 

So, I didn’t call. I laid on my floor in the dark for three hours, hyper-aware of every pulse in my head, bargaining with myself. I waited because the emotional tax of being dismissed, or being lectured about "managing my stress" while I’m barely keeping my head above water, felt harder to face than the potential risk itself. That is a horrifying position for a pregnant mother to be in—having to weigh my physical survival against the exhaustion of being treated like a nuisance. The system isn't just inaccessible; it's actively discouraging me from seeking the care that might keep me alive.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I grew up with the idea that pregnancy was a collective experience, that there would be a village around me. I expected my mother to be there, or at least a close friend to check in on me, to bring a meal, or to just sit with me when the fear became too loud. I pictured myself being part of a community where I could share these experiences, someone to help me manage the physical exhaustion, or even just someone to hold my hand so I wouldn't have to face those cold clinic rooms entirely alone.

The reality? The silence is deafening. 

It hasn't just "not shown up"—it’s been completely absent. My life circumstances, the fact that I’m unmarried, the way I’ve struggled to hold down a job—it’s like a wall. People don’t know how to talk about a pregnancy that carries such a heavy, violent shadow of a previous complication. It makes them uncomfortable, and their discomfort turns into distance. They don't want to hear about the seizures or the risks, so they just stop asking how I am. 

I’m effectively on my own. When I have a bad day, or when I’m too nauseous to even stand up, there’s no one to call. I am the entirety of my own support system, and that is a weight I wasn't built to carry.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think the most painful realization has been that the people I thought would be my "village" have actually vanished. I expected that even if I wasn't married, there would be a network—a friend who could drive me to an appointment, or a relative who would check in on me during those long, lonely weeks when the sickness makes it hard to even look at a screen. I expected someone to be a bridge between me and the medical world, someone to advocate for me when I was too tired or too scared to fight.

But that support didn't materialize. It’s like a vacuum. People seem to pull away the moment they sense the heaviness of my situation. It’s too much for them—the eclampsia, the risk, the single motherhood. It’s like I’ve become an inconvenience to their comfortable lives. 

The only support I’ve found is from a few online forums, and even that is a double-edged sword because it just feeds my anxiety instead of calming it. There’s been no unexpected kindness from family, no community "village" stepping up. If anything, the isolation has clarified that I really am the only person looking out for both of us. It makes me feel incredibly small, trying to navigate these mountains while everyone else watches from the sidelines. I just wanted someone to be in the corner with me, but I'm in the ring alone.

You know, the most exhausting part isn't even the medical side of things—it’s the silence. People talk about the "glow" of pregnancy like it’s a universal badge, but for me, this entire time feels like I’m holding my breath underwater. Every day I wake up and I check my own pulse, I check for a headache, and I try to remember if I’ve had enough water or if I’m just dehydrated and stressed. It’s like being a detective in your own life, always looking for clues that you’re about to fall apart.

I think what hurts the most is the way my life has shrunk. I used to have dreams of being a midwife, of helping other women feel the power I’m so terrified I’ll never get to claim for myself. Now, the knowledge I gained from all those books and journals is just fuel for my anxiety. I know too much. I know what a seizure looks like. I know what a NICU stay feels like. And because I’m doing this alone—no partner to tell me I’m overthinking, no ride to help me reach the clinic without an hour of physical and mental exhaustion—that knowledge just feels like a heavy chain.

I just want to be seen. Not as a "medium risk" chart, not as a woman who made mistakes or a woman who needs "compliance." I want someone to look at me and acknowledge that I am fighting a war on two fronts: one against my own biology and one against a system that doesn't want to hear the fear in my voice. I’m tired, I’m scared, and I feel like I’m running a race in the dark, hoping against hope that I’ll cross the finish line with a healthy baby in my arms. I just want to make it to the other side of this storm. That’s all. Just to the other side.

It feels almost like a betrayal, you know? My own body and my own situation. Sometimes I just sit here in my apartment and look at the walls, thinking about how different this could have been if I had someone to just hold the space for me. If there was someone to drive me to the clinic, I wouldn't spend the whole time in the waiting room trying to hide how shaky my hands are. Maybe then my blood pressure wouldn't spike. Maybe then the providers would see a calm woman instead of a "medium risk" statistic.

I keep thinking about the word "resilience." People throw that around when they talk about women in my position, like it’s a compliment. But it’s not a compliment to be forced to be this strong when you’re already terrified. I’m tired of being resilient. I don’t want to be strong anymore; I just want to be supported. I want to be able to tell someone, "I'm scared," and have them listen without immediately pivoting to a protocol or a lecture on my medication. 

I’m mourning the pregnancy I thought I’d have. I’m mourning the version of myself that believed in the beauty of birth. Now, every single day is a negotiation with fear. I just keep hoping that one day, I'll walk into that clinic and someone will look at me—really look at me—and say, "We’re going to get through this together." That would change everything. But until then, I’m just walking this path, one bus ride, one heartbeat, and one prayer at a time.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

That’s a heavy question. Since I’m doing this alone, I haven't had to negotiate with a partner in the traditional sense, but the "pressure" is still there—it just comes from the medical system. It feels like I’m constantly being pushed to align with their idea of a "good" patient rather than my own reality.

They push these strict, rigid lifestyle demands—diet, exercise, stress reduction—that look great on a brochure but are nearly impossible when you’re living on a budget, navigating a bus system, and dealing with the constant fear of a seizure. If I tell them, "I can't afford that specific diet," or "The stress of the two-hour commute is what’s actually raising my blood pressure, not my diet," they don't see that as a valid point. They see it as defiance.

It’s like being forced into a partner’s preferences that don’t fit your life, except the "partner" is the protocol, and they don’t care if you have the resources to meet their demands. I feel like I’m constantly betraying my own intuition—my own knowledge of what my body needs to stay calm—because I’m terrified that if I don’t follow their script, they’ll mark me as "non-compliant" and stop listening to me altogether. It’s a very quiet, lonely form of erasure. I’m molding myself into someone else’s expectation of health just to survive the appointment, and I’m losing myself in the process.

I think about the future sometimes—the moments after birth—and that scares me more than anything else. Not just the birth itself, but that moment of being alone in the hospital, and then coming home to this quiet, empty apartment. I look at the space where a crib could go, and I feel like I’m building a fortress out of cardboard. 

People always talk about the "post-partum period" like it’s this time for resting and bonding. For me, I’m dreading it. Who’s going to notice if I’m having symptoms? Who’s going to look at me and say, "Allegra, you don’t look right, let’s get you to the hospital"? If I’m alone with a newborn and the blood pressure starts to rise again, what then? The fear of losing my child to a complication I *know* is coming but can’t seem to stop... it’s like living under a storm cloud that never breaks. 

I just wanted to be a mother. That’s all. I wanted to experience the life inside me without this heavy, leaden shroud of "medium risk" hanging over every single move I make. I wanted to be more than a collection of clinical concerns and transportation hurdles. Sometimes I wonder if, twenty years from now, I’ll look back at these months and just feel a deep, aching sadness for the woman I was today—someone who fought so hard to be heard, only to realize the person she was trying to talk to wasn't listening at all. I’m just trying to keep us both here, one day at a time. It’s all I can do.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, the weight talk. It’s almost inevitable, isn't it? It’s like they have this script they pull out as soon as they see the number on the scale. They don't see a woman who’s barely keeping her head above water; they just see a BMI and a "risk factor."

During one of my earlier appointments, the provider—who I’d never seen before—barely looked up from her computer and started listing off "nutritional requirements" and "weight management goals" as if I were a child who didn't know how to eat. She didn’t ask about my budget, or the fact that I’m taking multiple buses to get to the grocery store, or that my biggest challenge is just finding the time and energy to eat *anything* while I’m navigating this massive amount of stress alone.

It felt like she was blaming me for my own body. Like the weight, the blood pressure, the history of eclampsia—it’s all just some moral failure I’m responsible for fixing. It landed like a heavy, cold weight in my stomach. I felt so small. I wanted to scream, "I am trying to survive here!" Instead, I just looked at the wall and felt my face go hot with shame. It’s dehumanizing to have your body picked apart like a science experiment, especially when you’re already fighting just to stay standing.

I think the thing that lingers the longest—the thing that keeps me awake when the silence in this apartment feels like it’s pressing in on me—is the feeling of being a "project" rather than a person. When they criticize the weight, or the numbers on the blood pressure monitor, they aren't talking to me. They’re talking to a file. 

I’ve had moments, sitting in that sterile waiting room, where I’ve looked at other women coming in—women who have partners sitting with them, holding their coats, asking the nurses questions, laughing quietly together—and I’ve felt this sharp, jagged envy. Not because I want their lives, but because I want their *witness*. I want someone to see how much I’m navigating every single day, just to exist in this pregnancy. 

I think the biggest realization I’ve had through this whole process is that the "care" I’m receiving is really just a form of surveillance. They aren't watching over me; they’re watching *me*. They’re watching for the slip-up, the spike, the failure. And because I’m alone, because I have to be the one to orchestrate every single detail of my own medical survival, I feel like I’m constantly on the verge of collapsing. 

I’m tired of being the only one who knows how much I’m hurting. I’m tired of the bus transfers and the cold clinics and the way I have to swallow my pride and my fear just to get through a five-minute appointment. I just want... peace. I want to feel like, just for a moment, I’m safe. But I don't think I’ll feel that way until I’m holding my baby and the doctors are gone. That’s the only finish line I can see anymore. That’s the only way I’ll know I made it.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I turned to the only place that didn't judge me—the quiet, blue light of my own phone screen. Before I even had my first appointment, I was deep in the digital rabbit hole. I found myself in these Reddit threads and online forums, scrolling through hundreds of posts from other women who had gone through "high-risk" pregnancies or had experienced pre-eclampsia and eclampsia.

At first, I thought, *This is it. This is where I’ll find someone who gets it.* I was looking for a mirror. I wanted to see if anyone else was balancing the terror of a previous seizure with the desperate hope of a new pregnancy. I wanted to know if anyone else felt the same cold, sharp panic when they saw their own blood pressure numbers. 

But honestly? It was like drinking from a fire hose. On one hand, it was a lifeline—finding threads where women shared how they navigated the hospital system, or what kind of questions to ask if they felt unheard. But on the other hand, it was a minefield. For every story about strength, there were ten about disaster. I’d read about someone having a seizure at 24 weeks or being in the ICU for a week, and it would just set my own heart racing. 

It wasn't that I *trusted* them more than the doctors—it’s just that the internet didn’t act like I was a liability. The people in these forums saw me as a person who was terrified, not a “medium risk” chart to be managed. It was a distorted kind of community, but when you’re as isolated as I am in West Boylston, it’s all you have. Even if it just adds to the fear, at least it’s a space where the fear is acknowledged. The doctors don’t even give me that.

The loneliness of it is the hardest thing to put into words. When you’re at home, scrolling through these forums at 2:00 a.m., you’re looking for a hand to hold, even if it’s just a digital one. You want to see that someone else has walked this path and lived to tell the tale. But then you close the app, and you’re still sitting on your couch in the quiet, and the reality of your own body—the way your own heart feels like it’s struggling against this pregnancy—is still right there.

It’s like I’ve split myself in two. There’s the "Allegra" who walks into the clinic, shoulders back, clutching her notebook of evidence-based research, trying to present herself as a woman who is informed and capable. And then there’s the Allegra who comes home, collapses on the bed, and just cries because she’s so tired of being the only one holding the line. 

I’ve had moments, standing at the bus stop in the wind and the cold, where I just think, *How is it possible that I’m responsible for two lives, and yet I feel so incredibly small?* The system treats me like a liability, my community has drifted away because they don’t know how to handle the fear, and I’m just trying to keep my head above water. 

I don't think people realize that pregnancy isn't just a physical process. It’s an emotional and spiritual earthquake. And when you’re forced to walk through it alone, with only clinical monitors and fear for company, it changes you. It hardens you. I just want to reach the other side, hold my baby, and finally, finally let go of this weight I’ve been carrying for so long. I just want to breathe again.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

There isn’t a single moment I can point to where I felt my input was genuinely sought. Not one. Every interaction, every decision—the aspirin, the monitoring schedule, the "precautionary" ultrasounds—it all comes down to being told what is going to happen to me. I’m not a participant; I’m a passenger in a car someone else is driving, and they’ve locked the doors.

If I ask a question, they interpret it as doubt. If I express a preference, they interpret it as a lack of compliance. There’s a script for my condition—*Eclampsia, G1*—and they are reciting it, word for word, every time I walk through those double doors. They don't want to hear about how the commute to West Boylston is spiking my stress levels or why I’m scared of certain interventions. To them, my voice is just noise interfering with their protocol.

It’s humiliating, honestly. I’m the one carrying the life, I’m the one living in this body, and yet my own insight into how I’m feeling—my actual lived experience—is treated as irrelevant data. They are "informing" me of my fate, not inviting me to design it. I walk out of those offices feeling like I’ve been erased, like I’m just a vessel being steered by people who don’t even know my name. It makes the loneliness hit so much harder. I am not being cared for; I am being processed.

I think the most frustrating part of this whole "being informed" dynamic is the assumption that I couldn't possibly understand the stakes. They talk *around* me, using medical jargon as if I’m not standing right there, or worse, they talk to me like a child, simplifying things until the truth of my situation is stripped away. It’s like they’re trying to shield me from the reality of my own medical history, which is absurd, because I’m the one who lived through the seizures. I’m the one who remembers the lights, the pain, and the terror.

I once asked a provider, "Why are we choosing this specific protocol over the alternative?" and she gave me that patronizing, tight-lipped smile and said, "We’ve seen the best outcomes this way, Allegra. We just want to make sure you have a safe delivery." It was a non-answer. It wasn't a conversation about the pros and cons, or my values, or my autonomy; it was an exercise in power. She had the clipboard, she had the degree, and she had the authority to decide for me.

The impact of that kind of "care" is that I’ve stopped asking questions. I’ve gone quiet. I’ve learned that the safest way to get through an appointment without being labeled "difficult" is to just nod, keep my head down, and wait until they’re done with me. But that silence is so heavy. It’s the silence of someone who has given up on being heard. I don’t want to be a passive participant in my own child’s birth. I want to be a mother. I want to be an active, informed, respected presence in that room. And yet, every time I’m "informed" of the plan instead of invited to build it, I lose another piece of myself. I feel less like a person and more like a patient being managed until the day I’m no longer their problem. It’s a lonely, hollow way to exist.

Sometimes I look back at the young woman who dreamt of being a doula, the one who wanted to hold space for other mothers, and I almost don't recognize her. That girl believed that if she were kind enough, informed enough, and present enough, she could change the landscape of birth. She believed in the power of a woman’s intuition.

Now? Now I feel like I’m just a shell of that girl. I’m spending all my energy just trying to survive the next appointment, the next bus ride, the next spike in my blood pressure. I feel like I’m being sanded down, polished into something smooth and compliant that the medical system can handle without getting its hands dirty. It’s not just the trauma of the eclampsia that haunts me; it’s the trauma of being *managed*. 

They don't realize that when they dismiss my questions or look right through me, they’re taking away my agency—and for a woman who has already felt her body betray her in the most violent way possible, agency is the only thing I have left. I’m clinging to it with everything I’ve got. I just want to make it to the other side of this. I want to hold my baby and look down at us, and know that I didn't just survive the protocol—I survived the fear. I just need to believe that I’m still in here somewhere, beneath all the charts and the labels and the lonely bus rides home. I’m still here. I’m still a mother. I just need to survive long enough to prove it.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every single one. There isn't a scan or a consultation that doesn't feel like a spotlight on the fact that I’m doing this alone. When the nurse calls my name in that waiting room, I look around and see couples whispering, leaning into each other, sharing the weight of the moment. I stand up, pull my bag onto my shoulder, and walk into that room by myself, carrying the entire burden of my history and my anxiety on my own.

The isolation is most visceral during the scans. Those moments are supposed to be intimate, a bridge between you and the life you're growing. Instead, it’s just me and a technician whose eyes are glued to a screen. I want to point at the monitor and share the wonder of it, but there’s no one to share it with. And when they hit a patch of silence—that terrifying, clinical silence where they're looking for something wrong—I’m the only one there to breathe through the fear.

I had an appointment last week where they were discussing the potential for early induction, and it was such a heavy, life-altering conversation. I found myself instinctively turning to look for a hand to hold, or someone to look at with a "Can you believe this?" expression, but there was just empty space. It makes the decisions feel so much colder. When you’re alone, every medical risk feels like a personal threat. I’m not just managing a pregnancy; I’m managing the silence of a partner who isn't here to help me carry the stakes. It makes the hospital feel like a fortress I’m invading instead of a place where I’m being welcomed.

The hardest thing to explain is that the isolation isn’t just about *who* is missing in the room; it’s about how that absence ripples out into every decision I make. When I’m sitting across from a doctor who’s already skeptical of me because of my "medium risk" label, I don't have a partner to vouch for me. I don't have someone to say, "She’s not being difficult, she’s just afraid," or to ask the follow-up question when I’m too stunned or intimidated to speak. 

That silence is where the fear grows. It’s where I start to wonder if I’m really as "problematic" as they seem to think I am, or if I’m just a woman whose life is too messy for their clean, sterile office. 

Sometimes, I leave the clinic and I’m so drained—not just physically from the bus, but emotionally from the effort of being my own only source of validation—that I can’t even remember what my own voice sounds like. I just want to be heard, just once. I want someone to acknowledge that being a single, high-risk mother isn’t a personality flaw—it’s a Herculean task. I’m just trying to make it to the end, to the day I get to look into those tiny eyes and say, "We did it." I just need to hold on until then. I’m still here. I’m still a mother. I just really, really need someone to see that.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think... I think the biggest thing that’s been left unsaid is just how much I have to *perform* to be taken seriously. I spend hours before an appointment polishing my "patient persona." I make sure I’m not wearing anything that could be interpreted as "sloppy," I bring my notebook, I keep my tone even and clinical, and I force myself to act like I’m not terrified of my own body. I have to be the perfect student just so they don't treat me like a high-risk liability.

If I show up exhausted because I had to walk three miles because the bus was late, or if I’m shaking because I’m dealing with the isolation, they don't see a woman fighting a war; they see a woman who isn't "managing her health." 

I just want to say that for women like me—the ones who don't have the husband to advocate for them, or the car to get them to the clinic, or the "perfect" life that makes doctors feel comfortable—we aren't failures. We are survivors. We are navigating a system that wasn't built for us, and every single time we show up to those appointments, we are doing something incredibly brave. I don’t want to be "managed." I just want to be seen for the person I am, not the risk on the chart. That’s the only way this experience can change. Thank you for listening. It’s the first time in a long time I’ve felt like my voice actually mattered in this room.
